system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 17: Augmented Cycles

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 17 — Augmented Cycles

_Pages 26-26._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Augmented chord construction

> Augmented chords are built on major thirds. A "C" augmented chord has the tones
> "C", "E" and "G#" (Exercise 38). When this chord is moved up a major third on the fin-
> ger board, the same tones appedr. See Exercise 39.

**Significance (p. 24).** Defines the augmented chord's intervallic structure and its key property — moving up a major third reproduces the same pitch-class set.

## Symmetric Augmented Cycle — three tonics

> Divide the 12 tone scale in three equal parts and you get three tonics, T. 1, 2, and 3.
> Exercise 40 demonstrates the Augmented chord ina Symetric Cycle.

**Significance (p. 24).** Establishes the core organizing principle: three-tonic equal division produces exactly three distinct augmented-chord tonics.

## Expanding to six tonics

> It is possible to use
> the'Augmented Cycle inmany ways. For instance, exercise 41 is similar to exercise 40,
> however, now we have six tonics, the additional tonics having been forced into the cycle.

**Significance (p. 24).** Introduces inserting additional intermediate tonics into the cycle.

## Symmetric Harmony — parallel motion rule

> Let me bring out this fact. Symetric Harmony, as we are applying it, moves from one
> point to another. What goes on between these two points is of no consequence. Harmony
> is parallel either up.or down and you can have as many as 12 tonics (Exercise 42).

**Significance (p. 24).** Governing rule for symmetric harmony: only start and end points matter, with strictly parallel motion and up to 12 tonics.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
